blocks.text: This species is known from just two collections in the Essequibo river basin, Guyana. There is insufficient data to confidently estimate area of occupancy (AOO) and its extent of occurrence (EOO) is highly uncertain but has been estimated to lie between 8–6,000 km2, with the lower bound based on specimen information only, and the upper bound based on the extent of the Kanashen Amerindian Protected Area in which both collections were made. No information on population was found and, due to the inaccessibility of the area, it is unknown whether this species is as rare as the herbarium record suggests. The collection area has recently been declared an IUCN Management Category VI protected area (Kanashen Amerindian Protected Area), however, the most recent collection of the species dates to 1989, thus pre-dating this. The Kanashen Amerindian Protected Area consists of largely inaccessible, near-pristine forest, and has an extremely low population density, therefore the impacts of human activity are mostly negligible. However, increasing accessibility to Guyana via the Takutu bridge and a new road infrastructure system will facilitate access to currently remote areas, which could lead to increased deforestation within the protected area if illegal mining and logging become prevalent, a trend observed throughout Guyana. Considering this threat, the species is estimated to occur in just two locations and is consequently assessed as Vulnerable D2; despite the currently negligible threats within the Kanashen Amerindian Protected Area, the potential for illegal extractive activity could rapidly drive this species to extinction. Research is needed to establish the current population size and trend, as well as monitoring habitat trends.

Considering this threat, the species is estimated to occur in just two locations and is consequently assessed as Vulnerable D2; despite the currently negligible threats within the Kanashen Amerindian Protected Area, the potential for illegal extractive activity could rapidly drive this species to extinction. Research is needed to establish the current population size and trend, as well as monitoring habitat trends.

blocks.text: This species is known from just two collections in the Essequibo river basin, Guyana, at elevations of 240–260 m asl. There is insufficient data to estimate area of occupancy (AOO), and the extent of occurrence (EOO) is also highly uncertain, estimated to lie between 8–6,000 km2.

There is insufficient data to estimate area of occupancy (AOO), and the extent of occurrence (EOO) is also highly uncertain, estimated to lie between 8–6,000 km<sup>2</sup>.

blocks.text_rich: Biogeographic Realm: Neotropical

blocks.text: The population size of this species is unknown, however, due to the paucity of herbarium records, it is thought to be rare. The Kanashen Amerindian Protected Area consists of 625,000 ha of near-pristine forest that is relatively inaccessible (Alonso et al. 2008), therefore it is possible that this species is more abundant than the herbarium record suggests.
blocks.text_rich: The population size of this species is unknown, however, due to the paucity of herbarium records, it is thought to be rare. The Kanashen Amerindian Protected Area consists of 625,000 ha of near-pristine forest that is relatively inaccessible (Alonso et al. 2008), therefore it is possible that this species is more abundant than the herbarium record suggests.

blocks.text: This tree is recorded from lowland forest in the Guiana shield, reaching 5–20 m in height. There is no specific information on how this species is dispersed, and knowledge of dispersal within Myrtaceae is largely limited to deductions made from fruit morphology (Nic Lughadha and Proença 1996), but a study on Myrtaceae in the Atlantic Forest showed that species from this genus are dispersed by birds and mammals (Pizo 2000, E. Lucas pers. comm. 2020).

Lucas pers. comm. 2020).

blocks.text: Forest cover in the Guianas is among the highest in the world, with 77–93% remaining in Guyana, most likely due to protected areas and lack of infrastructure (Plouvier et al. 2012). Despite this, deforestation is becoming an increasing concern in this area, with gold mining being the primary driver; in 2007, 62,970 ha of forest area was cleared for gold mining activities in the Guianas (Plouvier et al. 2012). Many protected areas overlap with gold reserves, putting these areas at risk of illegal mining (Plouvier et al. 2012). In addition to deforestation associated with gold mining, the process of extracting gold can have significant environmental impacts, including pollution of freshwater systems, which has already impacted the Essequibo river (Plouvier et al. 2012). The Konashen Amerindian Protected Area is inhabited by the Wai-Wai indigenous group, however the population density within the protected area is extremely low and most activity is small-scale (Alonso et al. 2008). Some deforestation for pasture has been observed the along Essequibo river (Wassenaar et al. 2007) and there are reports of extractive activities such as mining occurring within the park in the past (Alonso et al. 2008). Despite this, human impacts appear to be minimal as a result of its protection status and inaccessibility of much of the interior (Alonso et al. 2008). Potential plans to build a new road infrastructure system between Brazil and Guyana will facilitate access to currently remote areas, which could lead to an increase of the deforestation rates in future (Plouvier et al. 2012).
blocks.text_rich: Forest cover in the Guianas is among the highest in the world, with 77–93% remaining in Guyana, most likely due to protected areas and lack of infrastructure (Plouvier et al. 2012). Despite this, deforestation is becoming an increasing concern in this area, with gold mining being the primary driver; in 2007, 62,970 ha of forest area was cleared for gold mining activities in the Guianas (Plouvier et al. 2012). Many protected areas overlap with gold reserves, putting these areas at risk of illegal mining (Plouvier et al. 2012). In addition to deforestation associated with gold mining, the process of extracting gold can have significant environmental impacts, including pollution of freshwater systems, which has already impacted the Essequibo river (Plouvier et al. 2012). The Konashen Amerindian Protected Area is inhabited by the Wai-Wai indigenous group, however the population density within the protected area is extremely low and most activity is small-scale (Alonso et al. 2008). Some deforestation for pasture has been observed the along Essequibo river (Wassenaar et al. 2007) and there are reports of extractive activities such as mining occurring within the park in the past (Alonso et al. 2008). Despite this, human impacts appear to be minimal as a result of its protection status and inaccessibility of much of the interior (Alonso et al. 2008). Potential plans to build a new road infrastructure system between Brazil and Guyana will facilitate access to currently remote areas, which could lead to an increase of the deforestation rates in future (Plouvier et al. 2012).

blocks.text: The two known collections were recorded within an area which has recently been declared the Kanashen Amerindian Protected Area, however the most recent collection of this species was in 1989. There is no information on ex situ conservation measures for this species at present (BGCI 2021).
